title: Part 12
text: not properly closed, propelling a crisis situation further into more crisis (crisis is contagious); the second is when the schism is officially incorporated into the re-integration ritual phase: recognized and made public, ‘stated’ and staged, normalized into the emerging structure, rather than overcome. Turner says that he had noted such a bifurcation in his African fieldwork: ‘Either there was an overt reconciliation of the conflicting parties, or there was social recognition that schism was unavoidable and that the best that could be done was for the dissident party or parties to split off’ (1988: 104). What Turner does not mention is that in modern territorial states it is very difficult to ‘split off’. Anthropology and Sociological Theories of the Modern Before we move on from Part I of this book, let us provide one final set of explicit comments regarding analytical frameworks and the social theoretical drawing on anthropology. What we argue here evidently belongs to a dialogue between Liminality and the Modern 108 disciplines, between anthropology and sociology, and between ethnography and social theory. In this sense, this book is part of a larger project which seeks to explore the ways in which anthropologically inspired approaches can stimulate social and political theory. It is to be situated in the encounter between anthropology and sociological thinking, transporting an anthropological concept into the heart of the social sciences writ large. Such an interdisciplinary dialogue, which draws on anthropological insights in the theorization of the modern, has to be carried out with extreme caution. As we have argued elsewhere in more detail (Thomassen 2013a), it is indeed risky to apply concepts beyond the field of investigation in which they emerge. Ever since the inception of the social sciences, anthropology has constantly been used by social theorists as a starting point for the construction of alterity, a platform from which to think ‘the Other’ of modernity (Faubian 2000). Such a use of the ‘primitive’ does of course go back to some very central traditions in European philosophy and thinking – back to the Enlightenment, and back to seventeenth century contract theory – and its need of the exotic Other as a negative or positive reference point. Anthropology has often served to sustain various incompatible views of the ‘human’ or of ‘human nature’, to fantasize about an ‘original state’ of human affairs or ‘state of nature’ (Kuper 1988). Far from belonging only to Hobbes or Rousseau, Romanticism or speculative philosophy, this construction of alterity has been perpetuated within the allegedly most empirical and objective traditions in social theory, including functionalism and Marxism. The notion of an original primitive society was quite a central foundation for arguments made by Marx and Engels, which relied much on the then existing anthropological speculation on ‘origins’ that dominated early evolutionism. Anthropological works on primitive peoples stimulated Marx and Engels’ idea of ‘primitive communism’, a view of an undifferentiated group of people living freely together without centralized power or private property. Durkheim operated a much similar conceptual divide between mechanical and organic solidarity, instrumentally using dubious data on ‘archaic’ people to explain modernity. Even Freud saw anthropology (and the Australians once again) as a shortcut to establishing the universal rules of the deeper psyche (Kuper 1988: 105–22). These tendencies were later smuggled in through the back door by the leading social theorists across the Atlantic, most visibly in branches of social theory inspired by Durkheimian and/or Marxian approaches. In America, Parsons aimed to construct an evolutionary scheme of social development and social organization. Parsons basically posited primitive society as ‘undifferentiated’, at the social, cultural and personality levels (Faubian 2000: 252); and in this way ‘it’ still served as a starting point of difference, from where to articulate the unfolding modalities of differentiation that according to Parsons had guided social development (Parsons 1960). The primitive also takes centre stage in Habermas’ evolutionary narrative of communicative action. In Theory of Communicative Action [TCA] (1984, 1987), Habermas makes the overall argument that in modernity ritual has declined and been replaced by rational discourse. Habermas can make Dimensions of Liminality 109 this argument because, following Durkheim, he sees ritual as a primordial, original form of social communication about meanings. However, according to Habermas it is an ‘irrational form’, and very limited, because it does not allow for rational deliberation and consensus based on argument. As he says in volume II of TCA ‘[A] modern observer is struck by the extremely irrational character of ritual practices’ (1987: 191). Such irrational pre-modern forms of communication were supported by mythical thinking, which according to Habermas was ‘illogical’. Mythical thinking, says Habermas, ‘confuses internal relations of meaning with external relations among things [and] validity with empirical efficacy’ (ibid.). These illogical and irrational forms are overcome, in Habermas’ view, by a historical process of rationalization, which he sees as connected to a certain ‘linguistification’ of the sacred/ritual forms, whereby bodily forms of behaviour are replaced by reflexive, linguistic communication in open discourse. In volume 1 of TCA, Habermas discusses mythical thought from the very beginning, following his initial definition of rationality. What he says about primitive society and mythical thought constitutes the very platform of analysis. The problem is not only that Habermas misreads Lévi-Strauss and is vague and contradictory when he tries to indicate what anthropological material he is actually building upon in his evolutionary tale of rationalization; it is the nature of the whole enterprise which is problematic, in exactly the same way as Arnold van Gennep pointed out the untenable nature of Durkheim’s theoretical enterprise. Habermas’ theoretical construction is argued as an empirical account based on ethnographic ‘data’, but in reality it is achieved via a hypothetical procedure, a genuine thought experiment, a series of ad hoc deductions into which the primitives are made to fit as opposites – albeit, of course, granted an inbuilt human potential for mature rationality. In this sense there is a direct line of descent from Marx, Freud and Durkheim, to Parsons and Habermas; and it is a genealogy that leads us nowhere. Dialogues between anthropology and social theory have tended to develop within and between the dominant paradigms of the twentieth century: evolutionism, functionalism, structuralism, (post)Marxism, symbolic interactionism, post- structuralism and post-modernism. While this is not surprising, it does tend to limit the scope of the debates and for a very simple reason: the anthropologists who came up with genuinely novel ideas and concepts were precisely the ones who reacted strongly against those dominant paradigms and whose works cannot be understood within them. The concepts and ideas presented here, namely liminality, trickster logics, gift-giving and schismogenesis, were in fact not introduced by the most famous or dominant anthropologists. They were ideas sparked from an encounter between a Western observer and a non-Western cultural context, which in a few but significant cases led anthropologists to the conclusion that the language they had been given to think with was limited if not entirely erroneous. They therefore had to move their analysis outside the dominant intellectual paradigms. These anthropologists all somehow engaged in universalities and made in fact very daring arguments; at the same time – and this is essential to stress – they went up against any superficial usage of the ethnographic record. They strongly and flatly rejected Liminality and the Modern 110 the idea that all primitive societies were simply ‘the same’, just as they rejected the idea that members of such societies were without any degree of ‘individualism’ (this, as we saw, was one of van Gennep’s main charges against Durkheim) or that they were ‘undifferentiated’. In other words, without engaging with the hopeless project of pressing all cultures into preconceived theoretical schemes, categories or evolutionary utopias, they took their analyses to the level of cultural foundations, shared predicaments of humanity – and this is something quite different. We use the word ‘foundations’ in conscious distinction from ‘origins’. And rather than trying to erect developmental schemes based on substantial features or cultural or mental properties (or lack of these), the comparison they proposed had to do with shared forms. Arnold van Gennep, Marcel Mauss, Gregory Bateson and Victor Turner belong to this category of thinkers. They each in their own way made contributions that we can still draw from and together represent elements toward a base from which to build new bridges from anthropology to social theory. This will become clearer as we engage with the ways in which liminal conditions entered and diffused within the space of modernity. Part II On the Liminal Conditions of the times in Which We Live This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 5 Liminality in the Transition to Modernity: The Case of Thomas Hobbes and René Descartes The search for the ground, thus, remains recognizable as the reality experienced even in the modes of deformation. However much the symbolisms of deformation may express existence in untruth, they are equivalent to the symbolisms of myth, philosophy, and revelation. (Eric Voegelin, The Ecumenic Age) In the past, it was the work of the intellectuals to sublimate the possession of sacred values into a belief in ‘salvation’. The conception of the idea of salvation, as such, is very old, if one understands by it a liberation from distress, hunger, drought, sickness, and ultimately from suffering and death. Yet salvation attained a specific significance only where it expressed a systematic and rationalized ‘image of the world’ and represented a stand in the face of the world. For the meaning as well as the intended and actual psychological quality of salvation has depended upon such a world image and such a stand. (Max Weber, The Social Psychology of the World Religions) This chapter will approach the larger time period that followed what Huizinga termed the ‘waning of the Middle Ages’ in Europe from around the end of the fifteenth century to the mid-seventeenth century that is often referred to as ‘early modernity’. Early modernity, following here Huizinga and Voegelin, was not experienced as a ‘breakthrough’ or a passage toward a more ‘mature’ modernity, but rather as a total collapse of order, an age of anxiety, an ‘age of great confusion’ (Voegelin). In the terminology proposed here, it was a liminal age. The age was in desperate search for new ordering principles within politics, philosophy and science. Within this larger field of emergence, two very specific but also famous cases will be invoked: the live experiences and related thought systems of the contemporaries, Thomas Hobbes and René Descartes. In the confronting of liminality – the loss of taken-for-granted structures – the disorder of the early modern age was overcome with the ideational and political-institutional structure that was established with the ‘moment of Westphalia’, and with Cartesian science laying the foundations for modern thought. We argue that this new order was in a critical sense liminal at its core, jointly establishing fear and doubt (archetypical liminal experiences) as foundational. Liminality and the Modern 114 Hobbes and Descartes: Why and How to Read them Together Thomas Hobbes and René Descartes are rarely read together. There are obvious reasons why not. Thomas Hobbes developed a radically negative view of the human person on the basis of which a new political order was proposed. René Descartes developed a new and radical version of truth and science built upon the safe ground of the rational human self. Hobbes was one of a handful of prominent thinkers carefully selected to comment upon Descartes’ Meditations. Here Hobbes openly went up against some of Descartes’ most fundamental ideas, including the argument of the cogito itself. Descartes replied, and in no less harsh tones, that ‘as long as my critic refuses to come to terms with me about the meaning of words, none of his objections can be other than frivolous’ (Descartes 1997: 209). Taken at face value, Hobbes and Descartes talked past each other. It seems reasonable to conclude that Hobbes did not have a very high opinion of Descartes, and vice versa (Sommerville 1992: 20). It is also a commonplace to assert that the two thinkers were in fact occupied with two very different and incommensurable projects. Descartes’ main focus was not politics, and he proposed no theory of the state to compete with the Hobbesian construct. Hobbes, on the contrary, was highly involved in science, and was, just like Descartes, engaged in developing a general philosophy. However, we read Hobbes today almost exclusively because of his political ideas. Even if contemporaries considered Hobbes a scientist (Malcolm 2002: 51) his interest in geometry and optics is included in the literature on Hobbes mostly to the extent that it is regarded as having shaped his view of politics (for an exception, see Pacchi 1965). We treat the two thinkers as forefathers within two different realms: Hobbes is the founder of modern politics, while Descartes is the founder of modern science and philosophy. Put simply therefore, Hobbes and Descartes are rarely read together because they posited almost opposite philosophical anthropologies as the building stones of their thought, and because their works took off (or rather, were carried off) in different directions. The important connections between Hobbes and Descartes were taken up most explicitly by Richard Tuck (2002 [1988] and 1998). Tuck discussed how the two thinkers were drawn into the same academic landscape via the group of philosophers gathering around Mersenne, and indicated the extent to which Hobbes may have developed his philosophy through his reading of Descartes. Tuck also stressed how their projects resembled each other: they both wanted to establish a new philosophy, and they agreed that whereas human perception of the world was irremediably faulty, a new departure must be made by regarding sense-impressions as the object (rather than the basis) of investigation. Indeed, beyond their disagreements (which were indeed substantial), Hobbes and Descartes may very consciously have considered each other rivals in laying claim to this idea (Tuck 2002: 28–31). The idea was revolutionary because while accepting the scepticism of antiquity, it offered an alternative: a modern philosophy and a new scientific world image departing from the self. Hobbes and Descartes were not ‘too different’, but all too similar. Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 115 The argument we wish to propose here does not build on the actual correspondence between Hobbes and Descartes as this was mediated by their mutual friend, Marin Mersenne; nor will the connections between the two thinkers be biographically or textually traced. Rather than focusing on their substantial disputes or disagreements, it will instead be suggested that Hobbes’ and Descartes’ epistemological claims represent complementary ways of instituting modernity. The parallels between Hobbes and Descartes exist at the level of morphology, in the kind of ‘absolute’ boundary they draw around the modern state and the modern self, respectively. Their writings can be compared although the order that they establish is substantially different, and although they principally apply it to two very different kinds of ‘interior’: the sovereign state and the sovereign self. These underlying parallels, and the joint Weltbild they served to conjure, were captured by Borkenau in his landmark work, Der Übergang vom feudalen zum bürgerlichen Weltbild: Studien zur Geschichte der Philosophie der Manufakturperiode (Borkenau 1976 [1936]). Secularity in Modernity One of the landmarks of the modern world-view that developed from the seventeenth century was the introduction of secular principles in both thought and political organization, and Hobbes and Descartes are rightly seen as founders in exactly this sense: in the grounding of their systems of thought they abandoned, once and for all, a divinely-ordained legitimization. In seeking out a new ground, they both discovered a new type of principle which could supersede and replace religion and its dangerous divisive tendencies that had shattered Europe during the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. In most readings, Hobbes and Descartes are therefore also primary figures in the rationalization of thought and practice that paved the way for a wider secularization process, as a new boundary between the secular and the sacred would slowly become accepted and institutionalized. In this sense, the development of thought in the mid seventeenth century is rightly considered a forerunner to the Enlightenment, and both Hobbes and Descartes must beyond doubt be considered as path-breaking transitional figures toward European modernity. The debate between secular and religious interpretations of Hobbes has been raging since at least 1953, when Hood published The Divine Politics of Thomas Hobbes as a challenge to the more dominant secular interpretations, which tend to see Hobbes’ religious writings as opportunistic and in no way foundational for his real aim: creating a secular political theory built onto a philosophical materialism. However, the debate has been an integral part of the reception of both Descartes’ and Hobbes’ works ever since they were published in the seventeenth century. The exact terms of the debate have changed, but the main questions remain: is religion central to their systems of thought? If so, then how so? Are their religious writings really combinable with their science? Did Hobbes and Descartes really care about God and the Bible, or were they (consciously?) paving the way for the final victory of a secular, scientific world-view? Liminality and the Modern 116 These questions inform not only our understanding of Hobbes and Descartes: they pertain to the broader discussion of the religious foundations of modernity. Yet whilst Hobbes and Descartes certainly were masters of rationalization, neither of them actually introduced a separation between sacred and secular. Rather, they triumphantly introduced a new ‘secularity’ through a reorganization of Christian theology in the worldly realm of politics and thought. The Hobbesian state was secular only at one level, and the same can be argued of the Cartesian self. In what is presented here, the historical realities of specific types of state formation will not be discussed, as the focus is on thought symbolism. Even here the argument should not be seen as a contribution to the vast scholarship that exists on these two authors and their impact, as no new material will be presented. However, the commonalities pointed out may provide one further perspective from which to interpret existing facts. It will be argued that a breakthrough of ‘absolutism’ within both political theory and rational philosophy took place around the middle of the seventeenth century, 1 as exemplified in the work of Hobbes and Descartes, who, beneath their apparent differences and disagreements, shared to a substantial degree a common problem, to which they offered structurally similar solutions. These similarities can be spelled out by linking thought to experience, and to experience of a certain kind: human reactions to liminality. This chapter thus seeks to exemplify the general thesis that certain elaborations of liminal periods may prove decisive for the way in which a new order is established, at both the personal and societal levels, and that modernity on that account can in a crucial way be understood as a permanentizing of liminal conditions. Liminality in Political History: Linking Histories of the State with Histories of the Self Crudely speaking, the conceptual modelling of state and self are to be understood together because state formation is empirically related to a history of self- formation. The argument sustained in this chapter builds on literature that has analysed this very connection. In the Civilising Process (1994), Norbert Elias elegantly demonstrated how a specific kind of self-formation within the European court societies produced a fundamental and wide-ranging transformation of Western culture, which would affect the transformed, monopolized political rule called absolutism. As Oestreich (1982) showed in his analysis of the early modern state, the new characteristic of military discipline of the absolutist state grew directly out of the writings of the neo-stoic philosophers, and their concern with discipline, constancy and prudence. From the seventeenth century, the stoics gained new importance in the European history of ideas because they were interpreted as representing a pragmatic methodology of order, a secularized ethic of obedience brought to function, not by oppression, but by an active form of life 1 We had better make explicit that the term ‘absolutism’ (in inverted commas) is here and in the following invoked as referring jointly to state and self. Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 117 conduct through self-restraint. The ideas and practices of stoicism spread within both monarchies and republics, and within the principalities and cities composing the Holy Roman Empire, in the process of transforming themselves into fully-fledged states. At this critical moment in Western history, the stoics were read with renewed attention because of the perceived similarity between the public disturbances of ancient Rome and those of modern Europe. In this process, the political concern with order and the formation of subjectivity became interlinked (see Szakolczai 1999). 2 Both Hobbes and Descartes must be read with this linkage in mind. The widespread experience of disorder is crucial to the interpretation of Hobbes and Descartes. As used here, this amounts to something more than a ‘contextual reading’ of political ideas, if this is taken to mean the social and cultural ‘contexts’ in which, and from which, ideas develop. The transformation of the modern Western state system was, from the very outset, related to a process of social disciplining. The structural need for social disciplining arose in a large yet very specific context, namely with the dissolution of the mediaeval world order, and the collapse of Western Christianity as a representative of unity and order. The Reformation and its subsequent religious schisms and the religious wars that followed created a political and epistemological chaos that governed from the early sixteenth to the mid seventeenth centuries, when Europe was plagued by international war, civil strife and endless religious disputes. The absolutist states arose as a structured response to this prevailing disorder. As argued in previous chapters, such historical periods must indeed be thought of as truly liminal. Reality is experienced as contingent and uncertain, with lasting forms yet to be made. The anxiety and doubt that characterizes liminality is ultimately overcome by shaping the subject, who through a series of tests is brought to reflect on his or her self, in the search of a new identity and role to take up on the return to his or her society. There are indeed historical periods in which the social order is very fundamentally questioned and where widespread infra- communal violence and lack of social hierarchy renders normal interpersonal relations difficult if not impossible. This was a social collapse, culminating in a prolonged period of permanent religious and civil wars. The suggestion made here is that the early seventeenth century must be understood as a liminal period, 2 The work of Gerhard Oestreich and Elias’ analysis of the Civilizing Process share many resemblances with the analysis of subjectivity and subjectivation made by Michel Foucault. For example, when Foucault developed the concept of governmentality towards the end of his life, this was not due to any sudden shift of attention towards political theory or political rule per se, but because Foucault considered new forms of political rationality as reformulating the question of being. In Foucault’s own words, he saw the ‘technologies of self’ in early modern and modern Europe as being linked to the ‘political technologies of individuals’ underlying political rule (Foucault 1988, 1991). The preoccupation with constraint or self-control, as emphasized also by Elias, was transferred from private or fairly closed circles of human interaction to the wider society. Liminality and the Modern 118 out of which concrete practices and ideas emerged as answers to the crisis. Following Borkenau, it was those answers and their routinization form the critical underpinning of a modern world-view. The concept of liminality has its relevance to political and social theory precisely here, as it was developed by anthropologists to make sense of human experience and processes of subjectivation during moments of dissolution, in other words, during social and political crises; and such a perspective is equally applicable to the history of thought. The concept of liminality helps to bring into perspective the otherwise strange and very radical turn to self-foundation, evident in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. A founding characteristic of liminality is indeed to replace the outside chaos with an order of the inside, having radicalized and brought to exhaustion doubt and scepticism through personal and collective reflexivity. In the classical ritual passages to manhood one of the most common exercises is to force the initiands to cut off the sensory apparatus from the ‘world’ in order to gain an inward vision (often received in a dream-like state), a vision of truth that the initiand must elaborate and make manifest in the shaping of his new personality. As described by van Gennep (1960) and Victor Turner (1969), during ritual passages, the initiands or the neophytes are brought to question most radically their own sensory apparatus, just as they challenge social order by setting themselves apart from any normally accepted social rules from their betwixt and between position; they become nameless, timeless and socially unstructured, existing in a floating state of being, even as they acquire throughout the liminal period the necessary knowledge and experience in order that their transformed beings may eventually re-enter society and take up their new roles. It is by no means a coincidence that the driving principles in Hobbes’ and Descartes’ systems of thought relate back to two fundamental human experiences in liminality: fear (of violent death) and doubt (how can I know anything? What is my role in the production of truth?). The perspective adopted here is not entirely new; rather, the anthropological approach proposed speaks in evident ways to existing frameworks of analysis in political theory. The eschatological and ultimately millenarian structure of Hobbes’ political thought was, among others, emphasized in the analysis offered by J. G. A. Pocock. As argued by Pocock, Hobbes’ most radical innovation was to employ (and not delete) sacred history as a new instrument of secular power (Pocock 1989: 179; see also Martinich 1992). Methodologically, Pocock sought to establish a new type of history of political thought in which the available language and what could be termed the ‘taken-for-granted’ universe of the political thinker function as the basis of the textual interpretation. Pocock used as a theoretical basis the notion of paradigms, taking his cue from Thomas Kuhn’s The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. Pocock argued that political discourse has to be analysed by considering the paradigms within which a thinker poses problems and offers solutions (following here Kuhn, who said that the larger framework of scientific questioning was only very rarely put into question, and that ‘normal science’ was mostly a practice of puzzle-solving). By implication, Pocock argued that we have to analyse Hobbes by taking seriously the ‘intellectual paradigms’ within which Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 119 he lived and wrote (such as the common law tradition). This type of historical method was further developed by Quentin Skinner, and has indeed become an established tradition. However, to insist on ‘context’ never really answers what constitutes the context for the thinker himself, and in particular offers little aid in understanding thought at moments in which ‘paradigms’ (the available language and generally accepted ideas) are the very issue at stake. 3 With the concept of liminality, Pocock’s approach can be expanded to embrace not only language and intellectual paradigms, but also the wider field of human experience. This was exactly what was suggested by the political theorist, Eric Voegelin. Voegelin recognized that political thought had to be understood as symbolizations of real human experiences (see Voegelin 1974, 1990). Methodologically, Voegelin also recognized that thoughts are not simply second-order reflections of ‘reality’, but are themselves part of a historical process. Indeed, while Weber wanted to identify the ‘spirit of capitalism’, Voegelin tried to do the same, searching for the ‘moving forces’ in politics. Voegelin’s analysis of modern politics partly came to rest on his concept of ‘intramundane eschatology’, which has a strong affinity with Weber’s notion of ‘this-wordly asceticism’: in both cases a religious experience is immanentized as it comes to direct this-worldly action. It is with this approach in mind that the general life experiences during the crucial period in the development of the modern state, the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, need to be considered as the conditions of the emergence of the model of ‘absolutism’. Put simply, the crisis to which ‘absolutism’ became a response was first and foremost a religious crisis, and it touched upon the most basic relations between man and God, society and nature. While Pocock was certainly right that any interpretation of concepts must relate to their con- and inter-textual use, it can furthermore be suggested that more than setting ideas against established paradigms, the Hobbesian and Cartesian systems of thought must be placed at precisely the point at which paradigms are collapsing, and new ones are in the making. Therefore, understanding ‘absolutism’ is not possible only by looking historically and substantially at what it offered in terms of territorial, politico- administrative arrangements. Absolutism was a solution to a specific problem. Understanding the breakthrough of absolutism in the seventeenth century involves a look at the terms in which the problem was posed as a problem, and at the long- term consequences of these formulations. It is in precisely this genealogical sense that Hobbes and Descartes are founding figures: while their solutions quickly became objects of dispute, it was their problematization and their framing of possible answers which would remain the hidden foundations of the modernity they helped to install. 3 In a very real sense both Descartes and Hobbes were engaged in devising a new language departing from (and never moving beyond!) clearly defined concepts and ideas. Descartes even flirted with the idea of creating an artificial language (see 1629 letter to Mersenne, quoted in Gaukroger 1995: 201). Liminality and the Modern 120 René Descartes: Doubt and Certainty and how the Self became Mind But once I had spent some years studying [...] in the great book of the world and trying to acquire some experience, I decided one day to study also within myself, and to use all the powers of my mind
